questions.question: Define cryptographic hash function and list its three fundamental properties.
keyPoints: Hash function converts variable-length input to fixed-length output
Three properties: Pre-image resistance (one-way)
Second pre-image resistance
Collision resistance
Additional desirable properties: deterministic, quick computation, avalanche effect

questions.question: Explain the difference between MAC and Digital Signature with respect to keys used and non-repudiation.
keyPoints: MAC uses symmetric key (shared secret) - both parties have same key
Digital Signature uses asymmetric keys (private/public key pair)
MAC does NOT provide non-repudiation - either party could have created it
Digital Signature provides non-repudiation - only private key holder could sign
Non-repudiation means sender cannot deny signing the message

questions.question: What is the Birthday Attack? How does it affect the security of hash functions?
keyPoints: Exploits birthday paradox in probability theory
Without attack: need 2^n operations to find collision
With birthday attack: need only 2^(n/2) operations
For 128-bit hash (MD5): only 2^64 operations instead of 2^128
Reduces effective security by half the output bits
This is why 256-bit hash provides 128-bit collision resistance

questions.question: Describe HMAC algorithm with its formula and explain its security features.
keyPoints: HMAC = H((K⊕opad) || H((K⊕ipad) || M))
Uses nested hash structure with two keys (derived from K)
Provides security against birthday attacks (key blocks prevent collision search)
Provides security against length extension attacks (inner hash cannot be extended)
Can use any cryptographic hash function (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256)
Security relies on underlying hash function's collision resistance

questions.question: Explain the Avalanche Effect in hash functions and why it is important.
keyPoints: Avalanche effect: single bit change in input causes approximately 50% bits to change in output
Important for: preventing patterns in hash outputs
Ensures small input differences produce completely different hashes
Confirms good diffusion properties of hash algorithm
Helps prevent reverse engineering of hash function
NIST requires this property in hash standards

questions.question: Compare MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 algorithms with respect to output size and security status.
keyPoints: MD5: 128-bit output, cryptographically broken (collisions found in 2004)
SHA-1: 160-bit output, considered weak (collisions demonstrated in 2017)
SHA-256: 256-bit output, currently secure (no practical collisions)
All use Merkle-Damgård construction (except SHA-3)
Recommendation: Use SHA-256 or higher for new applications
MD5 and SHA-1 should not be used for security purposes

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'salt' in password hashing? How does it enhance security?
keyPoints: Salt is random data added to password before hashing
Prevents rainbow table attacks (pre-computed hash lookup tables)
Ensures same password produces different hashes for different users
Makes it impossible to use pre-computed databases
Should be unique per user and stored alongside hash
Does not affect password strength but protects against common passwords

questions.question: Explain the three types of resistance required for cryptographic hash functions with examples of attacks that would break each.
keyPoints: Pre-image resistance: Given hash h, find m such that H(m)=h. Attack: brute force 2^n attempts. Broken if feasible.
Second pre-image resistance: Given m1, find m2≠m1 such that H(m1)=H(m2). Attack: modify message slightly. Broken if easier than brute force.
Collision resistance: Find any m1,m2 such that H(m1)=H(m2). Birthday attack reduces work from 2^n to 2^(n/2). MD5/SHA-1 broken.
Relationship: Collision resistance implies second pre-image resistance but not vice versa
Practical impact: Collisions allow fake certificates (Flame malware used MD5 collision in 2012)

questions.question: Describe the MD5 algorithm structure and explain why it is no longer considered secure. Discuss the attacks that have been successfully mounted against it.
keyPoints: MD5 uses Merkle-Damgård construction with Davies-Meyer compression function
Four rounds using non-linear functions F,G,H,I with 32-bit operations
Process: padding → divide into 512-bit blocks → iterate compression function → produce 128-bit hash
Weaknesses discovered: Chosen-prefix collisions (2004), identical-prefix collisions (2007)
Practical attacks: Created fake CA certificate (2008), Flame malware used collision for code signing (2012)
Attack complexity reduced from 2^128 to 2^24 (chosen-prefix)
Conclusion: Should NOT be used for any security application

questions.question: Explain the message authentication process. How does it ensure integrity and authenticity? Discuss the limitations of using only hash functions for authentication.
keyPoints: Message authentication verifies: sender identity (authenticity) + message not altered (integrity)
Simple hash is vulnerable to modification attack: attacker can modify message and recompute hash
Solution 1: MAC - use shared secret key: MAC = H(M||K) or HMAC
Solution 2: Digital Signature - use private key to sign: Sig = H(M)^d mod n
Limitations of plain hash for auth: No secret key means anyone can compute hash of modified message
MAC limitations: Cannot provide non-repudiation (shared key)
Digital Signature limitations: Computationally expensive, requires PKI
Best practice: Use HMAC for symmetric authentication, Digital Signature for non-repudiation

questions.question: Describe the SHA-3 algorithm and explain how it differs from SHA-1 and SHA-2. Why was SHA-3 needed when SHA-2 was already secure?
keyPoints: SHA-3 uses Sponge construction (absorb→squeeze) instead of Merkle-Damgård
Based on Keccak algorithm (designed by Guido Bertoni et al.)
Different internal structure: uses permutation-based XOR, AND, NOT operations
Provides flexibility: can output any length (extendable output)
SHA-3 needed because: Different design philosophy provides security backup
SHA-2 and SHA-1 share similar structure (Merkle-Damgård)
If weaknesses found in SHA-2, having SHA-3 with different design is valuable
Both SHA-2 and SHA-3 are secure; SHA-3 chosen via public competition (2007-2012)
NIST standardized SHA-3 in 2015
